channel.title: Dalhousie Ocean Dynamics Group (DODG)
channel.description: Recent content on Dalhousie Ocean Dynamics Group (DODG)

item.title: Ruth Musgrave
item.description: I study the the physics of the ocean, mostly at scales that are relevant to the forward cascade of energy towards 3D turbulence and ocean mixing. I am interested in both the processes that lead to ocean mixing, as well as the impact that mixing has on the physics, biology and chemistry of the ocean.

item.title: Near-surface turbulence and air-sea gas exchange
item.description:
The rate of air-sea gas exchange for gasses such as CO$_2$ depend on both near-surface turbulence and the injection of bubbles into the surface layer of the ocean.

We are interested in understanding uncertainties in estimates of air-sea gas exchange of CO$_2$, and in particular how this affects model estimates of carbon drawdown associated with technologies such as marine carbon dioxide removal.

item.title: Clark Richards
item.description:
I am a physical oceanographer who uses observations to study how ocean water is mixed and transformed. I am currently working as Research Scientist at the Bedford Institute of Oceanography in Halifax, Nova Scotia. Previously, I worked as a physical oceanographer with RBR in Ottawa, Ontario, and as a postdoctoral investigator at the Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution in Woods Hole Massachusetts.

I obtained my PhD in physical oceanography in 2012 from Dalhousie University, working with Dr. Dan Kelley. I am also a co-author of the oce package for doing oceanographic analysis in R.

item.title: Marine Carbon Dioxide Removal
item.description: Marine Carbon Dioxide Removal aims to remove carbon dioxide from the atmosphere by storing it in the ocean. Techniques that we are interested include Ocean Alkalinity Enhancement and Direct Ocean Capture. The physical dynamics of the ocean play a crucial role in determining the efficacy of such interventions, through the competing effects of air-sea gas exchange, vertical mixing and subduction.

item.title: High latitude Ocean Mixing
item.description: We are interested in the physical processes that lead to turbulence and mixing at high latitudes, with a focus on the Arctic Ocean.

item.title: Internal waves
item.description: Oceanic internal waves primarily derive their energy from the winds and tides, but nonlinear interactions between waves shapes the nature of the internal wave background. Internal waves propagate throughout the ocean, carrying with them energy that will eventually be lost to turbulence and mixing.

item.title: Lina M. Rotermund
item.description:
I joined the Dalhousie Ocean Dynamics Group (DODG) in September 2020, when I began my PhD under the supervision of Dr. Ruth Musgrave and Dr. Clark Richards.

My research examines the processes that modify water masses passing through the Canadian Arctic Archipelago, specifically within Barrow Strait, which is included in the Tallurutiup Imanga National Marine Conservation Area. Oceanic, sea ice, and atmospheric properties have been intermittently monitored since 1998 at five locations spanning the channel by the Department of Fisheries and Oceans Canada. These measurements are used to investigate the processes that lead to water mass modification, including mixing through internal waves generated by tides interacting with the variable seafloor topography or by wind, and on surface processes such as formation of cold, salty or low-salinity waters as sea ice grows or melts.

item.title: Marie Babineau
item.description:
I am a graduate Student under the suppervision of Dr. Ruth Musgrave. My research focusses on the effects of depth varying current on lee wave propagation and energetics. I joined the group in 2020, after a bachelor’s degree in Water Engineering at Université Laval.

As for my hobbies, I play indoor and beach volleyball. I also enjoy trekking and rock climbing.

item.title: Ruby Yee
item.description: I am a MSc student currently working with Dr. Ruth Musgrave. My research focuses broadly on turbulence and mixing in the ocean and seeks to address questions like: what methods can be implemented to ensure that turbulence is quantified easily and accurately at sea? To what extent is mixing enhanced over topographical features like the Canada Basin shelf break in the Arctic Ocean? I am also working on a project closer to home—a numerical model of mixing within Bedford Basin. The model will provide insights into the specific situation of alkalinity release in the Bedford Basin surface layer. The carbonate chemistry and dispersal of alkalinity in the ocean is of particular interest, as ocean alkalinization has been proposed as one possible method for removing CO2 from the atmosphere. This work will support the Dartmouth company Planetary Hydrogen as they develop a carbon-negative method for hydrogen fuel production and alkalinity release.

Ocean dynamics

Ocean physics and its effects on ocean biogeochemistry

Oceanography is a naturally interdisciplinary field, bringing together biology, chemistry, physics and geology.

Bringing together ocean observations and models

Central to our approach is the use of both ocean models and numerical models.

Understanding the ocean and training a future generation of ocean scientists

To be completed

You can write $\LaTeX$ and Markdown here.
